title: Deputyship
text:
Deputyship - SupportGoWhere
Deputyship
Share link
For persons who lack mental capacity and have not made a Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA) and/or have no donee to make certain decisions on their behalf.
SERVICE HIGHLIGHTS
Fees payable
Referral not required
Collapse all sections
About this support

Deputyship is required if a person has lost mental capacity and did not appoint a Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA)

A deputy may be the patient's family member or friend

Who is this for?

Persons who have been certified to have no mental capacity.

What to expect?

You may apply to the court to seek a court order to be appointed as a Deputy.

Anyone above 21 years of age can apply to be a Deputy. A Deputy is usually a family member or close friend who would act in the best interest of the person who lacks capacity.

A doctor has to certify that the patient lacks mental capacity to make decisions due to an impairment of or disturbance in the functioning of the mind or brain

The Family Courts hear all deputyship applications under the Mental Capacity Act

Applicants may need to fulfil the services’ in-take criteria and providers’ service boundaries, if any.

How to apply?

Find out more on how to apply for a deputyship

Information last updated on 29 Sep 2025
